speaker: interviewer
text: Today is May 31, 1996. The survivor being interviewed is Sol Glick. His previous name was Galek. The interviewer is Judith Friedman Rosen, in Great Neck, New York. And the language of the interview is English. Today is May 31, 1996. Our-- our survivor is Sol Glick. And I'm Judith Friedman Rosen. We're in Great Neck, New York. And the language of the interview is English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good morning, Mr. Glick. Please tell me your name, and how you spell it, and where you come from.

speaker: interviewee
text: I come from Poland. My name-- my name was Sol Galek. Now when I came to America, I changed to Glick.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you spell Galek?

speaker: interviewee
text: G-A-L-E-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is your name-- and what was your Hebrew name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Schlomo.

speaker: interviewer
text: Schlomo. And when you say Poland, where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were born in Warsaw. What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1912, August the 8th.

speaker: interviewer
text: August 8, 1912. And did you live in Warsaw all your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My mother could not take the-- Warsaw, the-- the traffic and everything. We moved 15 kilometers from Warsaw in a small town.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Blonie, B-L-O-N-I-E. Blonie.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was your-- did-- did your mother come from Warsaw originally, or where did she come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my mother came from Koval, Koval. K-O-V-A-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was near the German border, Wroclaw, not far from Wroclaw, a big city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which today is Wroclaw, but in those days it was called Breslau right?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Wroclaw, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Also Wroclaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland, yes. It was near-- near the-- the German border.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so how did she get to-- how did she get to Warsaw? How did she meet your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, she met my father through my father's brother. He know my-- he know-- the family. He used to travel. And he introduced my father to my mother. And they got married after this, a couple months later.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know what year that was that they got married?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know my-- well, my brother was born in 1904. They must have got married about two years before, 1902.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this uncle that knew, the uncle that introduced your father--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes?

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind-- he was a traveling salesman, or how did he get around?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He get around-- selling anything. I don't know-- my father-- he left for America when I was not born yet. He run away from the Army. In Poland, you have to go, 21 years old, you have to go to register when you're 18. You're not allowed to leave. If-- if my uncle, my grandfather would be alive, he had to pay fine. Well my father was his brother. He paid the fine for him, two brothers escaped. They didn't want to go in the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they came to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Around what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh--

speaker: interviewer
text: This was before World War I?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes they-- they come to Chicago. My own brother-- my own uncle, my father used to tell me, was a professor in college.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in Chicago.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Chicago.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, And the other one was a tailor.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember as a child, did they write back and forth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the correspondence?

speaker: interviewee
text: My uncle who-- the professor was never married. He got killed by an automobile. He used to write and send packages to the family. After he got killed, we don't know what happened. My father used to wonder what happened to my brother. And then somebody came to visit in Poland, and told him the story, how your brother got killed. It was in the papers in Chicago for relatives for them to get the-- the money what he left. And this brother, got everything, [INAUDIBLE]. And he didn't notify us, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, and now this uncle who was killed, who was the professor who sent the packages, do you remember any of the packages that he sent? Do you remember anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. He sent presents for the sisters-- he had three sisters. My father come 13 brothers-- with 10 brothers and three sisters. And my-- my grandfather got married twice. He first wife died. He had seven children. And the second one is-- and I don't remember my grandfather. But I remember my grandmother when I was a baby. She-- she stayed with my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Galek, I don't know what's her name. I was a child. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you remember these presents? Anything special? Did he ever send you any present?

speaker: interviewee
text: Money, money, money he sent.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, he sent money.

speaker: interviewee
text: Money.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the family really need the money at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. My father didn't need it. But his sisters need it. My father supports the sisters too. They were still growing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, and your father did what?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was in the cattle business.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he have-- because you lived-- you lived in the village when he was in the cattle business.

speaker: interviewee
text: In a-- in a small town.

speaker: interviewer
text: In a small town. That's what we would call a shtetl.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, a shtetl.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember how many cows, or what kind of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, he had a lot of cows. I used to feed them and everything as a child. He used to have people going to-- every month was in another shtetl a sale. People come with the cows like a flea market. And he had people working for him. He told them what he needs, how many cows. He got orders from the farmers, and he-- he-- my father dealed with the Prince Radziwill too.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many cows did Prince Radziwill order?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, he has a big farm. He would not let in nobody to his farm, except, of course, my father. When my-- when my father's brother got killed by mistake in Europe, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my father was sitting Shiva. He would not go there, and he sent my mother, with a-- with a salesman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he would not let in the salesman there. But my mother could go in. But he took the cows out. The salesman man looked at them, and he bought it. And I never forget when he come back, my father told me, are you a salesman? He says, yes. You know how much his cows are worth, double the price. And you didn't know this? See, the prince told him a price. Later on-- So he-- so he had sold it for that price--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he sold it for the price for the prince ask him. But the price was very low. But with this cattles, he sold him pigs. And my father would not buy pigs. He was a very religious man. But he had to buy it. And my father sold the pigs to the butchers, the Gentile butchers for the same money. He said, I would not make a dollar on pigs. He was-- he was a very religious man. And this I-- I never forget.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, I'm just gonna jump ahead for a second, then we'll go back. But since your father had an association with the prince, during the war, did he ever try to bank in on that association-- or-- or by that time--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, through the war I hear that the prince-- I don't know what happened. His wife was alive, I-- when I came back. And she told the story of what happened. He was [INAUDIBLE] and everything. But his wife was still alive. And-- and his wife, I never forget when we came back through the war, and I-- and when I saw her, she said don't mention I was a prince.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, she didn't even want anyone--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no--

speaker: interviewer
text: Because she was afraid that that would cause a problem.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, don't mention. And she worked in the immigration. But a Communist already. And she was afraid. The Communists hated the-- the rich people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she said, don't you ever mention you know me. She was a lovely woman. But he was-- and she told how he got killed by the Communists and everything. The first thing the Communists killed all of the rich people.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So let's jump back. Tell us a little bit more about your father's cattle business.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, he was a--

speaker: interviewer
text: So, he sold-- he sold to farms, and he also sold--

speaker: interviewee
text: He sold it to the butchers, and to butchers.

speaker: interviewer
text: To-- to a [NON-ENGLISH], or to any kind of butcher? Did he mostly deal with Jewish butchers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: The main meat packing companies?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to gentile butchers and everything, yes. Yeah, he was a--

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have shepherds or people who took the cows from the fields-- from field to field? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. He just--

speaker: interviewer
text: --was it a farm?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We have special stables for the cows, for the milk and everything. And-- he sold which cows he had. He always had a lot of cows.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever out there milking the cows?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I used to milk the cows. My mother never went to the stable.

speaker: interviewer
text: No?

speaker: interviewee
text: She never know what a cow means.

speaker: interviewer
text: Really, after all that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I was the only one. I feed them and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well you had the-- you had more brothers. None of them--

speaker: interviewee
text: Four brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, you were five brothers. Do you remember any of the other brothers going out to--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The other brothers was-- one brother was in-- in business. My father put him in business. He bankrupt the business. And another time--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who bankrupt? Your brother bankrupt the business?

speaker: interviewee
text: The bankrupt--

speaker: interviewer
text: What business was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: He played-- he was a guy, he don't want to work. He wants to play pool and this for money. And later, when he got married, my father put him in a business again, and he bankrupt. But all the brothers helped him. He was one-- our brother-- we loved him. He was-- he was like a father. When we had trouble, we come to him to tell the trouble. We didn't tell my father. He would not care.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the younger brothers, did they go out to-- to-- to milk the cows too?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the younger brothers-- the-- one-- the brother which you see here in the wedding, he was a president in a bank. He had a bank. And the other brother was-- open a store. And the one brother who was got killed-- just died, he had a factory, making slippers and everything. And I used to deliver them to the stores. For orders, I used to take orders. And I used to help him working there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did-- did your mother's relatives ever come to visit you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because they-- they-- they were from far away.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my mother's sisters, three sisters lived in-- in the same city where we live.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, actually how far were you from Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 10 minutes-- 15 minutes by bicycle.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you really were--

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to-- I used to go by train. I used to go to school. I used to took a train. I have my monthly pass for the train. I could go all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Well, you didn't go-- you went to high school there. Where did you go--

speaker: interviewee
text: High school, I went in Blonie, in Blonie, the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait, first you went to elementary school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Elementary school, and later on, I went--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a Jewish elementary school, or was it a regular [? folk school? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: The first five years was a Jewish. And after five years, you go into a public school, with all mix, all nationalities.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Blonie?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Blonie, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Warsaw it was, you know, I tell you in Jewish, a Yiddish Gemeinde, a society for the Jews, they owned the school to teach the Jewish boys engineers, mechanics, tool makers. And I went to school there. It was in Warsaw. I used to go by-- by train.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there an organization that ran that school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. The Jewish organization, like you have a city hall, you know what I mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: Like the Jewish Gemeinde, the Jewish community.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish, everything the Jewish-- once there was almost a Jewish city. The religious people, they never spoke Polish. They speak with the Hasidim, with the payot. They own all the business. There was just-- they speak Jewish in the street. They never-- my-- my father never learned Polish, even his-- his name. He was a businessman. And I told him how to sign his name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, when he went to speak to the prince, didn't he speak to him-- he spoke to him--

speaker: interviewee
text: He speak to him in Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, in Polish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. Polish he could speak.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, he could speak the Polish, but he couldn't really write the Polish.

speaker: interviewee
text: He never went to school, and never wrote Polish. Even his name, I used to say, Pop, why did you sign his name?

speaker: interviewer
text: But your father, did he learn in a cheder, or where did he learn?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. He was-- in Hebrew, he was very educated, but not in Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- so you went to the Jewish school, and then you went to the public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And from there, you went into Warsaw to the Jewish school again?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which was a gymnasium.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was just like a gymnasium here. A professional, you learn everything. You finish there, you were educated. And you're a mechanic, or an engineer.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what-- what field did you learn?

speaker: interviewee
text: Electrical, plumbing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever use it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. When I was in California, that's-- that's what I-- that's what I took, my-- my profession. In Philadelphia, I was a photographer. I took a-- a job as a-- as a janitor. I didn't speak English.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. We'll get into that later. That's a different part of your life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but I went to California, in 1976, '75 they convert the apartment to condos, with a rush of people was-- real estate was very high. And-- and the apartment which I lived, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they sold the apartment to a Canadian company, kind of like Fairview. They're here in-- in Manhattan, a big real estate business. And they need a-- a manager. And I was supposed to move there. They gave me a relocation and everything. And I was supposed to move. And when the guy was looking at my house, making a telephone to the company. He said the manager, a woman, quitting. He needs a manager.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said, I can manage. I live here. I know the people. He said, how can we hire you when you were you fighting us not to throw the people out? You were going to court. I said, this is a different story. When I'm the manager, that's different. Right. OK. Let's go back to Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he made me the manager.

speaker: interviewer
text: Great. So then you can do your plumbing and your electrical.

speaker: interviewee
text: He gave me for three months, and I stayed there over 10 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, let's go back to Poland. Let's not jump so much, and-- and tell me. Did you-- when you went into-- when you went into Warsaw for school on the train, did you go with any friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you do with any friends from--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, by myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went by yourself. So from Blonie, you didn't have any classmates?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was just for boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know, but--

speaker: interviewee
text: And Warsaw was just for boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: You just went to Warsaw. But-- but-- were there other young Jewish fellows your age in Blonie?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I had a lot of friends, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: But, so did they come with you to-- to Warsaw, or they didn't go--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they lived in Warsaw. They come from every shtetl there. And then people from-- who lived in Warsaw. It was a big school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. But did any of the other boys from your town go to Warsaw with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, just me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you have to get accepted? Did you take a test?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I have to take a test, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. And do you remember if your parents discussed how much it cost or anything like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did it myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: You did?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know why? In this small time in shtetl, they used to have stores, pastry stores, and this. And I used to take orders. Instead of them going to Warsaw, bring after school I went into the stores, and I bought it. And they gave me a percentage.

speaker: interviewer
text: Really?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And sometimes, I came home from school about 2 o'clock. And I went back three, four times to Warsaw. I had a pass. And I-- instead of them traveling there, they didn't have time, they gave me the orders, and they gave me a percentage. That's why I made-- that's why I lived and paid everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of pastries were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, you have pastry. You have other kind of stores. And they need supplies.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no, I'm saying, what kind of pastry? Was it a different kind of-- of cake or something that we would know today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Cheesecake, danish, danish or bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they make danishes the same way we make them now? They looked a lot the same?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they were very delicious, the biggest stores there. Once it was-- the fifth city in-- in the world.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you brought them from Warsaw to Bronie?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Warsaw to the small towns. And there they gave me an order-- and later on they gave me some percentage.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's terrific. It's like a commission. A commission, they gave you a commission.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. That's what I did. Later on, when I worked with my brother, I helped him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, what-- what year did you finish in the high school? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it's a-- it's a big story. When I was a child, I got spinal meningitis, nine years old. And millions of people-- children died, thousands of children. And I was crippled. Do you know what spinal meningitis is? I was crippled. I had-- I had like this with a [NON-ENGLISH], and with-- with the-- with the iron on--

speaker: interviewer
text: On your legs.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the legs and everything, and I was blind. And they pronounced me dead. This is a true story. I was eight, nine years old. My younger brother, who you see there in the picture, I didn't walk. And he didn't walk either. He followed me. And my mother's sister lived next to us. And the doctor went into her, and told her, look, Mrs. Galek, he's not gonna live. Why the mother has to see-- he's gonna die. I want to give him a needle. That's why I believe in taking life. I give him a needle. And my mother find out, and she throw the doctor out. I don't want to see you. You want to kill my child. I do everything to save his life. Later, when the doctor came in, a couple of weeks later, pronounced me dead. I was dead. And you know, in the Jewish faith, the religious people, they put you overnight on the-- on the floor. And they put candles around. And the-- and the religious people who take care of the bodies. In the morning they wash him, and everything, and they take him to the cemetery.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when they were washing me, he put a mirror in my face, in my mouth. And he starts to scream, the baby's alive. They called the doctor. I just never forget, all my life, and the doctor said, he's alive. He is gonna go through hell. And nothing is gonna happen to him. He's going to be the strongest child in your family.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is a different doctor who said this.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The strongest child in your family. And he was right. I went through hell, operations, I heal right away. And of all my brothers, I'm-- I'm the only survivor. One died of a heart attack, one die here of cancer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now-- how did you-- how did you-- you strengthened your legs and all that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it took a long time. It took a long time. When I started going to school, I was already 10 years old. And I was going with the seven years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, that's why you were so old when you graduated high school, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That's right. But I-- I was so smart in one year, they push me three classes. That's true. I was very bright and everything. But anyway, I survived. And I always have in my mind what the doctor said. You can cut me here. The next day it's healed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me something. So that's where-- it's such a-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] such a contrast. Because you became a professional soccer player. When did you first get interested in soccer?

speaker: interviewee
text: In soccer, after about 11 years, 10 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: 11 years old-- 10 or 11 in school. So by then, you had already gotten rid of your braces?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I got the bracelets and everything. And when I was 18 years old, I had to register for the army. In Europe, you cannot leave. At 21 years old, like everybody complains, my heart and this-- they didn't want to go in the army. And I got an A, everything perfect. I survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was a soccer player, very famous and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- so-- so tell me, so what did that have to do with you graduating high school, the gymnasium, because-- because you graduated later.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I didn't graduate. I went to school there. After about 14, 15 years old I went to school. But I didn't graduate. I quit. And I help my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you quit, even though you were going--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was going at nighttime.

speaker: interviewer
text: To-- to the gymnasium in Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, nighttime, yes. Not the same school, the same school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait. You-- you went to school where you--

speaker: interviewee
text: A couple years.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went, and then you quit--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I could not afford to go there, I help my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went at nighttime to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went at night, right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's when you did the slippers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You helped him. And, but then you eventually did graduate? Or you didn't, not really?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- not-- not-- not, I didn't have the diploma. The war came, and I went to the army. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, by that time you were already 18?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, 18. And later when I went to the army, I came out for the army. I worked with my brother. And when the war came--

speaker: interviewer
text: Here we-- so actually, no, we're getting-- you-- you were in the army a year and a half before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Before the war, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the war, because you were drafted.

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1933, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were drafted.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, in Poland you have to go draft.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, at 18?

speaker: interviewee
text: 18 you have to register. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 18, you have to register. And 21 you go for a physical.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after the physical, then you go in the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, you went into the army.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And I had it good in the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, up to that time, were you religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was never religious.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were never religious.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was religious. He wants to make me a rabbi. And I want to play soccer. I could not stay. I was religious. I went to cheder and everything. But I was not an orthodox religious.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, when you went-- when-- when he went to shul, and you went to play soccer, what did your mother do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my mother-- oh, in the middle of the week, it was fine. But the-- the games was always on a Saturday. [LAUGHS] And I come back. My father come back. And my mother give me the meat, and the lunch. But he went to shul.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my father asked me, where-- where-- where is Sol? Oh, I gave him eat. He was hungry. And he must be outside. And I come back in the night for supper, and I'm sitting by the table. My head, and I said, pop, I saw in Warsaw an accident, a car and everything. Oh, my god, you went to Warsaw? You were in Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, he slapped me and everything. But he was a good father.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, how old were-- were you when that happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, about-- before-- before I went to the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is that when-- you went-- you went with Ha-Po'el? Is that--

speaker: interviewee
text: What? I played.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went to Ha-Po'el into Warsaw? Is that who took you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, yeah. I played soccer. In soccer you have to play as a child. You cannot play when you're older. After I was recuperating and everything, I start to play. And when-- when you get to 30 years old, 35, you can't still play. Here they play longer. But you have to learn from a child.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did you-- who started to interest you in playing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I was interest in playing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, I know. But what group? You didn't just pick up--

speaker: interviewee
text: Ha-Po'el-- first we had a working organization, people who work called-- later on, when I went in Warsaw, I joined the Ha-Po'el. And I played. I played.

speaker: interviewer
text: So besides doing sports with Ha-Po'el, what else did Ha-Po'el do?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: What else did you do with Ha-Po'el?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they-- they were all students, and everything. I used to go ice skating, and this, and running.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had socials?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. We were going together to dancing, and like young people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you ever have anything for Shabbat or any celebrations together, Jewish celebrations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, yes. All the Jewish holidays and everything, we used to celebrate very big. It's not like here. Our Passover was a week. Rosh Hashanah was a week, and Yom Kippur. [NON-ENGLISH] was-- and family was very happy there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And with Ha-Po'el, do you remember any songs? Did you sing any songs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I used to sing a lot of songs. But who remembers?

speaker: interviewer
text: More Zionist, right, songs about Israel? Do you remember were there any leaders who spoke about Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: I almost went to Israel by bicycle before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: You did?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my father was-- tried to kill me. They all-- all my friends went by bicycles. And they could not go through, you see, Russia would not let him go through Russia. Russia at the border, you can't go through. And they-- they took a boat. And they went-- to-- to-- to the-- later on-- they all went. I met them after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, so these friends from Ha-Po'el left--

speaker: interviewee
text: My friends-- my friends organized Zionists a trip by bicycle to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just not-- not to leave, just to go there to--

speaker: interviewee
text: To go to there, and they remained there. They were very-- very for Israel-- they were--

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- when they left, had they intended to remain there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father would not let me.
